title: शिव पञ्चरत्नम्
devanagariText:
॥ श्री शिव पञ्चरत्नम् ॥

नागेन्द्रहाराय त्रिलोचनाय भस्माङ्गरागाय महेश्वराय।
नित्याय शुद्धाय दिगम्बराय तस्मै नकाराय नमः शिवाय॥ १॥

मन्दाकिनीसलिलचन्दनचर्चिताय नन्दीश्वरप्रमथनाथमहेश्वराय।
मन्दारपुष्पबहुपुष्पसुपूजिताय तस्मै मकाराय नमः शिवाय॥ २॥

शिवाय गौरीवदनाब्जवृन्दसूर्याय दक्षाध्वरनाशकाय।
श्रीनीलकण्ठाय वृषध्वजाय तस्मै शिकाराय नमः शिवाय॥ ३॥

वशिष्ठकुम्भोद्भवगौतमार्यमुनीन्द्रदेवार्चितशेखराय।
चन्द्रार्कवैश्वानरलोचनाय तस्मै वकाराय नमः शिवाय॥ ४॥

यक्षस्वरूपाय जटाधराय पिनाकहस्ताय सनातनाय।
दिव्याय देवाय दिगम्बराय तस्मै यकाराय नमः शिवाय॥ ५॥

॥ इति श्री शिव पञ्चरत्नम् सम्पूर्णम् ॥
benefits: Sacred Shiva Pancharatnam for devotion and spiritual growth
Invokes blessings of Shiva in all forms
Traditionally recited for removal of sins and bestows spiritual merit
Ideal for Shiva festivals and daily worship
description: Among the most cherished prayers to Lord Shiva, Shiva Pancharatnam (शिव पञ्चरत्नम्) stands as five gems of devotional verses. This sacred composition is sourced from Purana Tradition and continues to inspire devotees worldwide. Chanting this stotra with devotion is said to invoke blessings of Shiva in all forms as well as remove sins and bestow spiritual merit. Additionally, it is known to grant divine protection and auspiciousness. Recitation is particularly auspicious on Monday (Somvar) and during Maha Shivaratri. With 5 verses and a reading time of about 3 minutes, this prayer is ideal for both daily worship and special occasions. On this page, you can read the complete Shiva Pancharatnam in Devanagari Sanskrit with English transliteration, Hindi meaning (arth), and free PDF download for offline recitation.

seoFaqs.question: What is the Shiva Pancharatnam and who composed it?
seoFaqs.answer: The Shiva Pancharatnam (श्री शिव पञ्चरत्नम्) — literally 'the Five Jewels of Shiva' — is a celebrated five-verse Sanskrit hymn in praise of Lord Shiva, traditionally attributed to Sri Adi Shankaracharya (8th century CE), the great Advaita master and prolific composer of stotras. The hymn is structurally identical with what is more widely known as the Shiva Panchakshara Stotram, in which each of the five verses ends with a salutation to one of the five sacred letters (na-ma-shi-va-ya) of the Panchakshara mantra Om Namah Shivaya. Each verse describes a distinct iconographic and devotional aspect of Shiva — Nagendrahara (serpent-garlanded), Mandakini-charchita (anointed with Ganga water), Shiva Gauri-mukha-sürya, Vasishtha-Kumbhodbhava-pujita, and Yaksha-svarupa-jata-dhara — making the composition a complete five-fold meditation. (Sources: Shankara stotra-mala; classical Shaiva tradition.)

seoFaqs.question: What is the meaning of Nagendraharaya Trilochanaya Bhasmangaragaya Maheshvaraya?
seoFaqs.answer: This famous opening verse — Nagendra-haraya Tri-lochanaya Bhasma-anga-ragaya Maheshvaraya, Nityaya Shuddhaya Dig-ambaraya Tasmai Na-karaya Namah Shivaya — offers obeisance to Shiva through eight epithets that build into a complete dhyana. Nagendra-haraya — to the one whose garland is the king of serpents (Vasuki); Tri-lochanaya — the three-eyed; Bhasma-anga-ragaya — whose body is anointed with sacred ash (vibhuti); Maheshvaraya — the great Lord; Nityaya — the eternal; Shuddhaya — the pure; Dig-ambaraya — the sky-clad (the digambara renunciate); Tasmai Na-karaya — to that one who is the syllable Na (the first letter of the Panchakshara). The verse closes with Namah Shivaya — salutations to Shiva. The whole verse is structurally a meditation on Shiva as the embodiment of Na, the first sacred syllable. (Sources: classical Adi Shankara stotra tradition.)

seoFaqs.question: How do the five verses build the Panchakshara mantra Na-Ma-Shi-Va-Ya?
seoFaqs.answer: The structural genius of the Shiva Pancharatnam (also called Shiva Panchakshara Stotram) is that each of its five verses concludes with 'Tasmai X-karaya Namah Shivaya' — a salutation to one successive syllable of the supreme Panchakshara mantra Om Namah Shivaya. Verse 1 ends with Tasmai Na-karaya — salutations to the Na-syllable form of Shiva (Nagendrahara, Trilochana). Verse 2 ends with Tasmai Ma-karaya — salutations to the Ma-syllable form (Mandakini-charchita, Mandara-pushpa-pujita). Verse 3 ends with Tasmai Shi-karaya — salutations to the Shi-syllable form (Shiva, Sri Nilakantha, Vrishadhvaja). Verse 4 ends with Tasmai Va-karaya — salutations to the Va-syllable form (Vasishtha-Kumbhodbhava-pujita, Vaishvanara-lochana). Verse 5 ends with Tasmai Ya-karaya — salutations to the Ya-syllable form (Yaksha-svarupa, Jata-dhara, Pinaka-hasta). Reciting the five verses is therefore a complete walk through the Panchakshara mantra. (Sources: Adi Shankara stotra-mala; classical Panchakshara tradition.)

seoFaqs.question: Is the Shiva Pancharatnam the same as the Shiva Panchakshara Stotram?
seoFaqs.answer: Yes, in most editions and traditions the two titles refer to the same composition by Sri Adi Shankaracharya. 'Shiva Pancharatnam' literally means 'the five jewels (pancha-ratnam) of Shiva,' while 'Shiva Panchakshara Stotram' means 'the hymn of Shiva built on the five-syllable (pancha-akshara) mantra' — both names describe the same five-verse hymn beginning Nagendraharaya Trilochanaya. Some classical anthologies use 'Shiva Pancharatna' for a slightly different five-verse hymn whose verses do not necessarily end on the Panchakshara letters; readers should check the specific verses to identify which composition is meant. The Shiva Panchakam (a separate composition opening each verse with Om Namah Shivaya rather than ending with a Panchakshara letter) is yet a third related five-verse Shiva hymn. (Sources: Shankara stotra-mala publications; classical Shaiva tradition.)

seoFaqs.question: What is the meaning of Mandakini-salila-chandana-charchitaya?
seoFaqs.answer: This is the opening of the second verse — Mandakini-salila-chandana-charchitaya Nandishvara-pramatha-natha-Maheshvaraya, Mandara-pushpa-bahu-pushpa-supujitaya Tasmai Ma-karaya Namah Shivaya. Word-by-word: Mandakini-salila — the waters of the Mandakini (the celestial Ganga that flows through Shiva's matted hair and through the sky); chandana — sandalwood paste; charchitaya — to the one who is anointed with (these); Nandishvara — Nandi the bull, Shiva's vehicle; Pramatha-natha — the lord of the pramathas (Shiva's gana attendants); Maheshvaraya — the great Lord; Mandara-pushpa — flowers of the celestial mandara tree; bahu-pushpa — many other flowers; supujitaya — to the one who is well-worshipped (with these). The verse worships Shiva as the cool, sandalwood-anointed, flower-bedecked Lord of Kailasa surrounded by his ganas, and offers salutations to him as the Ma-syllable of the Panchakshara. (Sources: Shiva Pancharatnam verse 2; classical Shaiva commentary tradition.)

seoFaqs.question: When and how should the Shiva Pancharatnam be recited?
seoFaqs.answer: The Shiva Pancharatnam is short (only 5 verses, about 3 minutes), making it ideal for daily nityapaath. Classical tradition recommends: (1) Recitation at Brahma muhurta after morning bath, sitting on a clean asana facing east or north before a Shiva-linga, picture or yantra; (2) Lighting a ghee lamp and offering bilva patra, water, akshat, vibhuti and white flowers; (3) Reciting Om Namah Shivaya as preliminary japa, then the Pancharatnam slowly with concentration on each verse and the corresponding Panchakshara letter; (4) Daily recitation as nitya-karma. Especially powerful occasions: every Monday (Somvar — Shiva's day), every Pradosh tithi (twilight of trayodashi), Maha Shivaratri (Phalguna Krishna Chaturdashi — the supreme Shiva festival), the entire month of Shravan, Krittika nakshatra, Kartik Purnima, and any Mahabhisheka or Rudra-abhisheka ceremony. (Sources: classical Shaiva nityapaath paddhati.)
